chunks.title: Health Issues and Vegan Diet
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses personal health issues, specifically asthma and eczema, and questions whether these conditions were exacerbated by a vegan diet. They reflect on the perceived health benefits of veganism versus their own experiences of not finding relief from symptoms.
chunks.transcript: so did you have the asthma and eczema like your whole life and then it was exacerbated by vegan diet or it was kind of just like all stable and you was wondering what caused it to begin with because this supposedly healthy vegan diet didn't fix it sort of thing

chunks.title: Introduction to Dietary Changes
chunks.summary: The speaker shares their journey with different diets, including paleo and the inclusion of various foods such as meats, vegetables, nuts, and medicinal mushrooms. They recount their experiences with severe eczema flare-ups despite adhering to what is broadly considered a healthy diet.
chunks.transcript: washington i was eating like a paleo diet so i was eating meat i was eating vegetables i was eating nuts and seeds and i was eating some quote-unquote medicinal mushrooms i was like uh these sort of probably hydrolyzed mushroom extract powders and so i was getting moderate doses of mushrooms and i had the worst eczema flare of my life those are just connected temporally but i was having eczema flares all through medical school i was doing a lot of jiu jitsu in medical school so i'd get it on my wrists and my elbows and i couldn't do jujitsu i was getting infected i was chunks.title: Curiosity About Alternative Diets
chunks.summary: After encountering the dietary approaches of figures like Jordan Peterson, the speaker reflects on their own beliefs about nutrition, particularly the role of vegetables and the traditional functional medicine perspective that emphasizes plant-based diets. They express curiosity about re-evaluating their diet towards more meat-centric options.
chunks.transcript: talking about his autoimmune disease and how doing his diet was fascinating i thought that's cool i've never tried that my my second thought was that's crazy because everyone knows that vegetables are good for you and they have all these phytochemicals and at the time i was interested in what's broadly called functional medicine and within the functional medicine paradigm vegetables are great eat the rainbow eat more vegetables and everybody knows more vegetables increases the quality of your microbiome and so i was kind of steeped in those ideas and the original notion that you could just eat meat was you know it counted all those it was very contradictory to what i was you know sort of steeped in at the time but i got curious

chunks.title: The Plant-Animal Diet Perspective
chunks.summary: The speaker presents a philosophical perspective on the evolutionary relationship between animals and plants, emphasizing that animals can defend themselves while plants have developed toxins as defense mechanisms. This prompts contemplation on whether plants might contain substances that could be harmful to some individuals.
chunks.transcript: started on the experiment because when i took a moment and i thought about the perspective of plants and animals it kind of crystallized for me and this is just this idea that nothing on earth wants to get eaten but an animal can run away from you kick you or bite you and so they don't seem to have defense chemicals in their muscles i mean occasionally like puffer fish tetrodotoxin or you know this poisonous frog in the amazon but 99.8 percent of animals and mammals and little reptiles are edible um but plants are completely different plants are they're stuck in the ground so 450 500 million years of co-evolution between animals and plants plants have had to develop defense chemicals many of which are quite beneficial within medicine today some of which we use as medications or chemotherapies but there's a lot of chemicals in plants they're like these chemical factories and there's been this warfare between plants and animals for generations you know millions of years hundreds of millions of years and some people hypothesize that the the sort of the the cyp systems the sip 450 systems in the liver are this evolutionary adaptation to plant toxins as we're evolving like how do we you know we're going to detoxify these plant compounds we have to hydrolyze them we have to glucuronidate them we have to oxidize them we have to get rid of toxins in our body so it makes sense like maybe plants are containing toxins that are problematic for humans and then the overarching sort of broader hypothesis would be what if some people are more sensitive to these plant toxins and that could be a trigger for autoimmune disease and that's sort of
